arc_summary: The structure is **interlaced rather than blocked**: the first purpose statement (s10) lands early, immediately gets justified by a returning gap (s11), and from there the introduction **cycles repeatedly through M1 -> M2 -> M3**. Each cycle pairs a territory review (LLM vs. human language, foraging, entrainment) with a contrast that distinguishes **dyadic word generation**, then announces a corresponding aim, framework, or measure. Multiple M3 segments accumulate — the explore/exploit lens, the similarity measure, the **individuation effect** — so the contribution is built incrementally rather than declared once. The recurring rhetorical engine is the same move each time: review what's known, mark how this task differs, derive a study construct from the difference.

move_roles.role: Establishes the territory: AI-human creative collaboration as a real phenomenon, then reviews the contrast between LLMs' narrow associative output and humans' multi-timescale, flat-hierarchy creativity. Names the 'flat associative hierarchy' construct the paper will draw on.
Early purpose statement — declares the study targets the hybrid human-LLM dyad — placed before the gap is even spelled out.
Retroactively justifies the just-stated aim: prior work covered fully-human and fully-LLM dyads but never the hybrid case.
Adds context that human-AI collaboration's net value is unsettled, motivating the inquiry.
Sharpens the gap into a concrete deficiency: LLM creative output is consistently less semantically diverse, evidenced across essays and creative writing, with social stakes.
Reviews the collective-foraging literature on how individual search style shapes group diffusion, supplying the explore/exploit grounding.
Positions dyadic word generation as an extension of foraging work to a two-agent case, framing the hybrid condition as the means to probe search-style diversity.
Returns to territory: meaning arises from interactive interplay between minds, grounding the task in interactive-language theory.
Defines the central task construct — the alternating single-word co-creative game — and asserts its value as a real-time trace of two minds through lexical space, with a worked example.
Cites prior work in this exact paradigm showing AI word generation diverges systematically from the human baseline.
Marks the residual gap: even steered LLMs miss the human balance of coherence and volatility, posing the open question the study addresses.

Introduces the explore/exploit analytical framework, distinguishes the task from foraging/category-recall paradigms, and operationalizes exploration vs. exploitation via cosine-similarity runs and drops — the methodological core.
Reviews the entrainment literature: speakers bring distinct lexical identities that align only gradually through communicative negotiation.
Distinguishes the task from communicative collaboration — no convergence pressure means individual lexical identities can persist rather than being negotiated away.
Derives the study's prediction from that distinction and names a new construct, the 'individuation effect,' as the measurable signature.

Closes by reviewing the contrasting LLM case — a single statistical model with no independent lexical identity and a tendency to anchor to surface context — setting up why the individuation effect should be absent in pure-LLM dyads.

sentences.text: Human creativity is a collective achievement, and even conventionally solitary creative acts increasingly involve the participation of one or many AI (often LLM-based) partner(s).
LLMs are driven by high-probability associations derived from common patterns in the training corpora.

As a result their words tend to concentrate within a relatively tight range of locally similar responses, steepening their 'associative hierarchy' (Mednick, 1962) and producing a narrower range of creative responses than humans (Chen & Ding, 2023; Mahowald et al., 2024).
Human language production, by contrast, operates hierarchically across multiple timescales (Caucheteux et al., 2023).
sentences.move_note: contrast tag here contrasts known properties, still reviewing territory

sentences.text: Human creativity in general, meanwhile, has been shown to alternate dynamically between convergent and divergent solution-generating strategies (Malaie et al., 2024; Nijstad et al., 2010).
Predictive weight in human language judgments is distributed across multiple levels of abstraction rather than concentrated only at the local level (Heilbron et al., 2022).
Human creativity in language use is therefore characterized by a probability distribution in which no single response dominates.
sentences.note: asserts a substantive characterization with independent content, not bare synthesis

sentences.text: In Mednick's terms, this is the 'flat associative hierarchy,' in which remote associations become more accessible.
sentences.note: names/introduces the construct the paper will use

sentences.text: Through joint interaction, pairs and groups navigate shared conceptual terrain and explore a common space of possible expressions without explicit instruction or reward (Williams et al., 2025).

The present study aims to investigate not how the collaborative creative dynamic of human and LLM-powered participants change in a hybrid context where both work together to generate words in sequence.
sentences.move_note: early purpose statement, intro then cycles back to M2/M1

sentences.text: Prior work has characterized word generation in fully human and fully LLM dyads, but the hybrid case, in which a human and an LLM work together, has not yet been examined.
sentences.move_note: return to gap after early M3 statement; M2->M3 cycling

sentences.text: Evidence for whether human-AI collaboration is a net positive remains mixed (Anderson et al., 2024; Vaccaro et al., 2024).
However, LLM-generated creative content consistently shows less semantic diversity than human-generated content.

This gap emerges quantitatively across human vs. LLM-generated college admissions essays (Moon et al., 2025), and human expert evaluation confirms a parallel quality gap in creative writing (Chakrabarty et al., 2024).
sentences.note: provides evidence/examples of the gap asserted in [13]

sentences.text: The downstream consequences of this creativity gap between human and AI-generated content have the potential to pose significant social challenges.
sentences.move_note: centrality framing of the gap's importance

sentences.text: In collective foraging, individual search style determines how well groups diffuse across new territory.
Garg, Kello & Smaldino (2022) modeled this directly, simulating agents who balanced individual exploration against the exploitation of social information.
Exploratory individuals spread out with minimal overlap, covered ground quickly, and kept the collective from converging on patches that were already depleted.
When individual search was exploitative, groups snowballed onto the same locations, exhausted them, and lost the diversity of coverage on which productive collective search depends.
Dyadic word generation extends this work by enabling us to investigate a two-agent case in which partners may differ substantially in their individual tendency to diffuse.
A hybrid condition enables us to ask how that diversity in search style shapes the collective's dynamics.
Meaning in language, also, does not emerge from individual minds working in isolation, but from the dynamic interplay between them (Clark & Brennan, 1991; Pickering & Garrod, 2004).
Dyadic word generation is a co-creative task in which pairs take strictly alternating turns producing single words.
sentences.note: defines the central task construct of the paper

sentences.text: Each individual is prompted by their partner's most recent response, and the task functions as a minimal co-creative language game.
sentences.note: unpacks the task introduced in [23]

sentences.text: Every word in the context of this task is a real-time measure of where two minds are in a wide-ranging trajectory through lexical space.
sentences.note: asserts the significance/relevance of the task measure

sentences.text: In one session, for example, a pair produced: Plane, flight, Bird, sky, Clouds, rain, Water, ocean, Fish, marine, Biology, ecosystem, Nature, forest, Trees, roots, mountain, trail —eighteen words arcing through a single sustained region of very lush and green lexical space— before the sequence broke abruptly into a double-meaning and, from there, a category shift — mix, blend.
sentences.note: illustrative example of the task described in [25]

sentences.text: Prior work comparing human and LLM dyads in this paradigm has found that AI word generation behavior differs systematically from the human baseline (Tanzer, 2024).

While LLMs could, in fact, be steered toward greater divergence, even then, they failed to replicate the dynamic balance of local coherence and semantic volatility that characterizes human-produced word sequences.
What, then, drives these differences, and what happens when a human and an LLM work collaboratively as a single dyad?
sentences.note: poses the open question the study addresses

sentences.text: Dyadic word generation can be understood through the lens of explore/exploit dynamics — a framework that has been shown to organize behavior across foraging, decision-making, learning, and creativity (Hills et al., 2015; Mehlhorn et al., 2015).
sentences.note: introduces the analytical framework of the paper

sentences.text: In lexical space, partners alternate between exploitation — concentrated sampling within a local region of related meanings — and exploration — departure toward new, more distant areas of the space.
sentences.note: defines explore/exploit as applied to the task

sentences.text: This profile of behavior resembles category recall, long studied as a foraging task (Hills et al., 2012, 2015), but dyadic word generation is less restrictive, with no fixed category requirement and no time or performance pressure.
sentences.note: distinguishes the task from prior foraging paradigms

sentences.text: Our task therefore allows pairs to roam the lexical space more freely.
sentences.note: makes a substantive comparative claim continuing the distinction in [32], not a bare synthesis

sentences.text: Moreover, responses are partner-cued rather than internally driven.
sentences.note: asserts a distinguishing feature of the task

sentences.text: Unlike paradigms where foraging yields an explicit score, the reward structure here is intrinsic.

Experiences of insight and semantic resolution engage neural reward circuitry (Oh et al., 2020), analogous to the dopaminergic responses that accompany harmonic resolution in music (Belfi & Loui, 2019; Williams et al., 2025).
sentences.note: cross-domain claim with citation support

sentences.text: The semantic similarity between words at different times in the series functions as our quantitative measurement of participants' movement through the lexical space (Johnson et al., 2023; Mikolov et al., 2013; OpenAI, 2022).
sentences.note: describes the measurement approach of the present study

sentences.text: Bouts of exploitation appear in the sequence as continuous runs of high cosine similarity.
sentences.note: operationalizes exploitation in terms of the measure in [37]

sentences.text: These arise from clustered series of words drawn from the same area of lexical space.
sentences.note: continues unpacking the measure; parent is [37]

sentences.text: Exploratory transitions are the drops in similarity that punctuate them when a move away from the local cluster is made.
sentences.note: operationalizes exploration; parent is [37]

sentences.text: The sustained arc through sky, water, and forest in our opening example is one such cluster; mix, blend was the transition out.
sentences.note: illustrative example tied to the measurement; parent is [37]

sentences.text: Human language production is deeply personal.
Each speaker brings a distinct lexical identity to any exchange as a result of their particular life experience (Brennan & Clark, 1996; Brown-Schmidt et al., 2015; Isaacs & Clark, 1987).
When human pairs begin communicative collaboration, they enter with distinct cognitive models and idiosyncratic description schemes that do not immediately align (Garrod & Anderson, 1987).
The process of entrainment is progressive, emerging only across many exchanges as partners negotiate a shared framework.
In dyadic word generation there is no such pressure to converge because there is no communicative demand that forces alignment.
sentences.note: distinguishes this task from communicative collaboration in [44]-[45]

sentences.text: Individual lexical identities are free to persist throughout the sequence rather than being negotiated away.
sentences.note: unpacks the consequence of the contrast in [46]; parent is [46]

sentences.text: If partners respond by drawing from overlapping but distinct lexical spaces, we predict that each person's words should be more semantically similar to their own prior words than to their partner's.
sentences.note: states a prediction/hypothesis of the study

sentences.text: This asymmetry is detectable in the structure of the shared sequence.
sentences.note: extends the prediction in [48]

sentences.text: We term this the individuation effect — a measurable signature of distinct cognitive identity within a jointly navigated lexical space.
sentences.note: names a new construct the paper introduces

sentences.text: An LLM generating both sides of a dyad, by contrast, draws from the same statistical model with no independent lexical identity to contribute.
Both 'partners' respond from the same internal distribution, with no variation in available vocabulary.
sentences.note: unpacks the claim in [51]

sentences.text: Moreover, previous work has also revealed a tendency on the part of LLMs to anchor tightly to user behavior and immediate surface context.
This is evidenced in audience
sentences.note: sentence truncated; appears to begin an illustrative example of [53]
